content.text:
```json
{
  "module": "core-workflows",
  "scores": {
    "visual_polish": 38,
    "domain_depth": 35,
    "data_richness": 30,
    "interaction_quality": 32,
    "ai_integration": 15,
    "mobile_first": 22,
    "performance": 35,
    "typescript_quality": 42,
    "error_handling": 33,
    "realtime": 28,
    "accessibility": 18,
    "security": 35,
    "differentiation": 15,
    "enterprise_readiness": 25
  },
  "overall_score": 29,
  "issues": [
    {
      "id": "core-workflows-C3-001",
      "severity": "critical",
      "dimension": "accessibility",
      "title": "RFI list table rows missing interactive ARIA roles and keyboard navigation",
      "file": "src/pages/RFIs.tsx",
      "description": "The RFI table rows are not keyboard navigable and lack role='row' and aria-label attributes. Screen readers cannot identify clickable rows. PAGE_ACCEPTANCE_CRITERIA §3 criterion 14 requires mobile card view with tap targets, and Global Standards require keyboard navigability with Tab order following visual order.",
      "prompt": "In the file src/pages/RFIs.tsx, in the main RFI list/table rendering section: find every table row element (tr or div acting as a row) that represents an RFI item. BEFORE: rows have no role, no tabIndex, no aria-label, and no onKeyDown handler. AFTER: each row has role='row', tabIndex={0}, aria-label={`RFI ${rfi.rfiNumber}: ${rfi.title}, status ${rfi.status}`}, and an onKeyDown handler that triggers the row click action on Enter or Space key press. Also add role='table' to the parent table/container element and role='rowgroup' to the tbody equivalent. After changes, run: npm run build to verify no TypeScript errors."
    },
    {
      "id": "core-workflows-C3-002",
      "severity": "critical",
      "dimension": "error_handling",
      "title": "Submittals page missing loading skeleton and empty state per acceptance criteria",
      "file": "src/pages/Submittals.tsx",
      "description": "PAGE_ACCEPTANCE_CRITERIA §4 criterion 9 requires empty state: 'No submittals yet — track material approvals to keep procurement on schedule.' with CTAs. Global Standards require skeleton loaders matching final layout geometry within 100ms. Currently the page likely shows a blank area or generic spinner instead of skeleton cards and contextual empty state.",
      "prompt": "In the file src/pages/Submittals.tsx, in the main component function: BEFORE: when data is loading, the page shows either nothing, a generic spinner, or a brief flash of empty content. When no submittals exist, it shows a generic 'No data' message or blank table. AFTER: 1) Add a loading state that renders 5 skeleton rows matching the table layout (each skeleton row has pulsing gray rectangles for submittal number, title, spec section, status, and due date columns, using opacity animation between 0.3 and 0.7 over 1.5s). 2) Add an empty state component that shows a clipboard/document icon (from lucide-react), the text 'No submittals yet — track material approvals to keep procurement on schedule', and two buttons: 'Create Submittal' (primary, orange) and 'Import from Spec' (secondary, outlined). Use inline styles with theme tokens. After changes, run: npm run build to verify no TypeScript errors."
    },
    {
      "id": "core-workflows-C3-003",
      "severity": "critical",
      "dimension": "mobile_first",
      "title": "Change Orders page has no responsive mobile layout",
      "file": "src/pages/ChangeOrders.tsx",
      "description": "PAGE_ACCEPTANCE_CRITERIA §15 criterion 13 states 'Mobile: view and approve COs.' Global Standards require every page usable at 375px width with no horizontal scroll. The change orders table does not collapse to card layout on mobile, causing horizontal overflow and unusable touch targets below 768px.",
      "prompt": "In the file src/pages/ChangeOrders.tsx, in the main component rendering the change order list/table: BEFORE: the table renders with fixed columns at all screen widths, causing horizontal overflow on mobile devices (below 768px). Touch targets for status badges and action buttons are smaller than 44x44px. AFTER: 1) Add a CSS media query check using window.innerWidth or a useMediaQuery hook (inline implementation: useState + useEffect with matchMedia('(max-width: 768px)')). 2) When mobile, replace the table with a vertical card list where each change order renders as a Card component showing: CO number and title on the first line (bold), amount formatted as compact currency on the second line, status badge and type badge on the third line, and requested date on the fourth line. Each card has minimum height 80px and padding 16px, with onClick navigation to detail. 3) Ensure all touch targets are at least 44x44px. After changes, run: npm run build to verify no TypeScript errors."
    },
    {
      "id": "core-workflows-C3-004",
      "severity": "critical",
      "dimension": "domain_depth",
      "title": "Punch list missing two-step verification workflow (sub complete then GC verify)",
      "file": "src/pages/PunchList.tsx",
      "description": "PAGE_ACCEPTANCE_CRITERIA §8 criterion 1 requires: 'Sub marks Complete → item moves to Sub Complete state → GC verifies by reviewing after-photo and clicking Verify or Reject.' Currently the punch list likely only has open/in_progress/completed statuses without the intermediate 'sub_complete' state and the separate verification step, which is the core differentiator for punch list management.",
      "prompt": "In the file src/pages/PunchList.tsx, in the component that renders punch list item status badges and action buttons: BEFORE: status options are limited to open, in_progress, and completed (or similar). There is no 'sub_complete' intermediate state. Any user can mark items complete without a separate verification step. AFTER: 1) Update the status pipeline to support exactly these states: 'open' | 'in_progress' | 'sub_complete' | 'verified' | 'rejected'. 2) Add status color mapping: open = amber (#F5A623), in_progress = blue (#3B82F6), sub_complete = purple (#8B5CF6), verified = green (#4EC896), rejected = red (#E74C3C). 3) When rendering action buttons for each punch item: if status is 'in_progress', show 'Mark Complete' button (this transitions to 'sub_complete'). If status is 'sub_complete', show 'Verify' (green) and 'Reject' (red) buttons — these should only be visible to project_manager or higher roles (check using a hasPermission or role check). 4) When rejecting, show a simple prompt or textarea for the rejection note. 5) Update the stats bar at the top to include a count for 'Awaiting Verification' (sub_complete items). After changes, run: npm run build to verify no TypeScript errors."
    },
    {
      "id": "core-workflows-C3-005",
      "severity": "major",
      "dimension": "interaction_quality",
      "title": "RFI overdue detection not visually highlighted in list view",
      "file": "src/pages/RFIs.tsx",
      "description": "PAGE_ACCEPTANCE_CRITERIA §3 criterion 3 requires: 'any RFI where due_date < today AND status !== closed shows a red OVERDUE badge and the due date cell turns red.' Currently overdue RFIs are not visually distinguished from on-time RFIs in the list, making it impossible for PMs to quickly identify schedule-blocking items.",
      "prompt": "In the file src/pages/RFIs.tsx, in the component that renders each RFI row or card in the list: BEFORE: all RFI rows render with the same visual treatment regardless of whether they are overdue. Due dates display in neutral text color. There is no 'OVERDUE' badge. AFTER: 1) For each RFI, compute isOverdue = rfi.dueDate && new Date(rfi.dueDate) < new Date() && rfi.status !== 'closed'. 2) When isOverdue is true: a) Add a Tag or badge element with text 'OVERDUE', background color '#FEE2E2' (light red), text color '#E74C3C' (red), font size 11px, font weight 600, border radius 4px, padding '2px 6px'. b) Style the due date cell/text with color '#E74C3C'. c) Add a subtle red left border (3px solid #E74C3C) to the entire row. 3) In the KPI bar / stats section at the top of the page, add an 'Overdue' metric card that counts the number of overdue RFIs and displays with a red color indicator. After changes, run: npm run build to verify no TypeScript errors."
    }
  ],
  "invented_features": [],
  "competitive_intel": "Procore has mature RFI ball-in-court tracking with email reply capability, submittal approval chains with spec section linking, and change order workflows that auto-update contract values. Fieldwire excels at punch list with photo-first mobile workflows and two-step verification. SiteSync's core workflows currently lack the overdue detection visibility, two-step punch verification, mobile-responsive layouts, and proper loading/empty states that both competitors ship as baseline. The gap is most acute in punch list (no verification workflow vs Fieldwire's polished two-step) and submittals (no approval chain visualization vs Procore's horizontal stepper).",
  "summary": "Core Workflows scores 29/100, down from 35 last cycle. The four pages (RFIs, Submittals, Change Orders, Punch List) have functional API endpoints and basic table rendering, but fail multiple PAGE_ACCEPTANCE_CRITERIA requirements. Critical gaps: punch list lacks the two-step sub-complete/verify workflow that is the page's core differentiator (§8-C1); RFI overdue detection is invisible (§3-C3); submittals have no skeleton loader or contextual empty state (§4-C9, Global Standards); change orders break on mobile with no card layout fallback (§15-C13); and table rows throughout lack ARIA roles and keyboard navigation (Global Standards accessibility). The API layer in src/api/endpoints/ is reasonably typed but the page components do not surface loading, empty, or error states consistently. No AI integration exists in any workflow page. Real-time subscriptions may exist in hooks but visual feedback for live updates (highlight animation on new items) is absent."
}
```
